content:
# Leviticus Chapter 2 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 58r–58v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_002_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_002_page_002.jpg`; **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 86–87 (LXX) | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_002_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_002_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And when a person [nefesh] brings near an offering [qorban] of grain offering [minchah] to the LORD, fine flour [solet] shall be his offering; and he shall pour oil upon it and put frankincense [levonah] upon it.

**2** And he shall bring it to the sons of Aharon, the priests; and he [the priest] shall take from it his handful [qomtso] of its fine flour and of its oil, together with all its frankincense; and the priest shall burn [ve-hiqtir] the memorial portion [azkarah] upon the altar -- a fire offering [isheh], a pleasing aroma [re'ach nichoach] to the LORD.

**3** And the remainder of the grain offering [ha-noteret min-ha-minchah] shall be for Aharon and for his sons -- most holy [qodesh qodashim] from the fire offerings of the LORD.

**4** And when you bring near an offering of a grain offering baked in the oven [tanur], [it shall be] unleavened ring-cakes [challot matsot] of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers [riqquey matsot] smeared with oil.

**5** And if your offering [is] a grain offering on the griddle [al-ha-machaват], [it shall be] fine flour unleavened, mixed with oil.

**6** You shall break it into pieces [pittota otah pittim] and pour oil upon it; it [is] a grain offering.

**7** And if your offering [is] a grain offering in the pan [ha-marcheshet], of fine flour with oil it shall be made.

**8** And you shall bring the grain offering that is made from these [things] to the LORD; and one shall bring it near to the priest, and he shall bring it near to the altar.

**9** And the priest shall lift [ve-herim] from the grain offering its memorial portion and shall burn [it] upon the altar -- a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**10** And the remainder of the grain offering [shall be] for Aharon and for his sons -- most holy from the fire offerings of the LORD.

**11** Every grain offering that you bring near to the LORD shall not be made with leaven [chametz], for any leaven and any honey [devash] you shall not burn [taqtiru] from it as a fire offering to the LORD.

**12** As an offering of firstfruits [reishit] you may bring them near to the LORD, but they shall not go up upon the altar as a pleasing aroma.

**13** And every offering of your grain offering with salt [melach] you shall salt [titmalach], and you shall not let the salt of the covenant [melach berit] of your God be lacking [tashbit] from upon your grain offering -- upon all your offerings you shall offer salt.

**14** And if you bring near a grain offering of firstfruits [minchah bikkurim] to the LORD, roasted [qaluy] grain [aviv] crushed [karmel] you shall bring near as your grain offering of firstfruits.

**15** And you shall put oil upon it and place frankincense upon it; it [is] a grain offering.

**16** And the priest shall burn [ve-hiqtir] from it its memorial portion -- from its crushed grain and from its oil -- together with all its frankincense; a fire offering to the LORD.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 58r–58v)

1. **v.1 -- Nefesh / "a person":** The Hebrew subject is נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh), meaning "a soul / a person / a living being." This is the same word used throughout the Torah for a human individual. The choice of nefesh rather than ish ("a man") or adam ("a human") is striking and deliberate -- the rabbinic tradition famously interprets this as indicating that even the poorest person, who can only bring a grain offering rather than an animal, has laid their very soul [nefesh] upon the altar. The LXX (Vaticanus p. 86) renders ψυχή (psuche, "soul"), preserving the force of the word. I render it "person" with a bracketed note rather than "soul" to avoid implying that the soul is what is being sacrificed, but the nefesh reading is noted.

2. **v.1 -- Minchah:** The word מִנְחָה (minchah) has a broader semantic range than "grain offering." In pre-priestly usage it denotes any gift or tribute (cf. Gen 32:14, Jacob's minchah to Esau; Gen 4:3-5, the minchot of Cain and Abel). In the priestly legislation of Leviticus 2, it becomes technically restricted to grain-based offerings, as distinguished from animal offerings (olah, zebach shelamim) and other categories. I render it "grain offering" consistently in this chapter, with the Hebrew at first occurrence. The LXX renders θυσία (thusia, "sacrifice") throughout -- a generalizing term that flattens the grain-specific character.

3. **v.1 -- Solet / "fine flour":** סֹלֶת (solet) is the finest grade of milled wheat flour, sifted of bran. The term distinguishes this offering from coarser flour (qemach). The requirement of solet signals that the grain offering, though the "poor person's" offering, is not inferior in quality -- only the finest flour is acceptable. The LXX renders σεμίδαλις (semidalis), the Greek term for the finest grade of wheat meal, a precise rendering.

4. **v.2 -- Qomtso / "handful":** קֹמֶץ (qomets, construct: qomtso) denotes a specific handful formed by folding the fingers closed over the palm -- the amount that fits in the palm of one hand. It is a technical priestly measurement term. The root q-m-ts appears primarily in priestly contexts. I render it "his handful." The LXX reads δράκα (draka, "a handful / a fistful"), a reasonably close rendering.

5. **v.2 -- Azkarah / "memorial portion":** אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) is one of the most theologically weighted technical terms in the grain-offering legislation. From the root z-k-r ("to remember"), it denotes the portion of the offering that is burned on the altar -- literally "the remembrance portion" or "that which causes remembrance." This small portion (the handful of flour, oil, and all the frankincense) represents the whole before God, with the remainder going to the priests. I render it "memorial portion" and transliterate at first occurrence. The LXX renders μνημόσυνον (mnemosynon, "memorial / remembrance"), a precise and close rendering that confirms the root meaning is ancient.

6. **v.3 -- Qodesh qodashim / "most holy":** קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים is the superlative construction "holiness of holinesses" -- "most holy." This designation marks the priestly portion of the grain offering as belonging to the highest category of sacred food, which may only be eaten by male priests within the sanctuary precincts (cf. Lev 6:14-18, where eating restrictions are elaborated). The LXX renders ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων (hagia ton hagion, "holy of holies"), the same superlative construction.

7. **v.4 -- Tanur / "oven":** The תַּנּוּר (tanur) is a clay or earthen oven, typically a cylinder set into the ground or freestanding, heated by fire from below or within. This verse introduces the first of three cooking methods for grain offerings: (a) baked in the oven (vv.4-5), (b) on the griddle (vv.5-6), (c) in the pan (v.7). The structural parallelism of the three subsections mirrors the structural parallelism of the three animal-category subsections in Leviticus 1. The LXX renders κλίβανος (klibanos, "baking oven"), the standard Greek term.

8. **v.4 -- Challot matsot / Riqquey matsot:** Two sub-types of oven-baked offerings are specified: (a) חַלֹּת מַצּוֹת (challot matsot), "unleavened ring-cakes / thick unleavened rounds" -- the challah is a thick ring or disk; (b) רִקִּיקֵי מַצּוֹת (riqquey matsot), "unleavened wafers" -- raqiq denotes a thin, crispy sheet. The distinction is between thick cakes and thin wafers. "Mixed with" (belulot) means the oil is incorporated into the dough; "smeared with" (meshuchim) means the oil is applied to the surface after baking. The two techniques of oil application are thus distinguished in the same verse.

9. **v.5 -- Machavat / "griddle":** מַחֲבַת (machavat) is a flat metal griddle or pan. The form of the Hebrew word is difficult; the dagesh in the beth suggests it may preserve an older form. The Leningrad Codex vocalizes it consistently with the massoretic tradition. The LXX renders τηγανιστά (teganista, "fried/griddle-cooked things"), a reasonable functional rendering.

10. **v.6 -- "You shall break it into pieces" (pittota otah pittim):** The verb p-t-t (piel: pittota, "you shall crumble / break into pieces") combined with the noun pittim ("pieces / crumbs") creates a figura etymologica -- "you shall piece it into pieces." This instruction distinguishes griddle-baked grain offerings (which are broken into pieces) from wafers (which are apparently served whole or folded). The LXX renders κλάσεις αὐτήν (klaseis auten, "you shall break it"), capturing the verb without the figura etymologica.

11. **v.7 -- Marcheshet / "pan":** מַרְחֶשֶׁת (marcheshet) is distinguished from the machavat (griddle) of v.5. The marcheshet is a deeper vessel -- a pot or deep pan that allows the grain mixture to be cooked in more oil, producing a softer, moister product than the flat griddle. The distinction between griddle (flat, crispy, less oil) and pan (deep, moist, more oil) is captured in later rabbinic discussion. The LXX renders ἐσχάρα (eschara, "brazier / grill"), which actually suggests the griddle type -- the LXX may not preserve the same culinary distinction.

12. **v.11 -- Chametz / "leaven" and Devash / "honey":** Two substances are prohibited from all fire offerings to the LORD: (a) שְׂאֹר (se'or) / חָמֵץ (chametz) -- both words appear in the verse, with se'or referring to leavening agent (sourdough starter) and chametz to any leavened product; (b) דְּבַשׁ (devash, "honey") -- this prohibition of honey is unexpected and has generated interpretive discussion. "Honey" here likely refers broadly to fruit syrup or date honey (dibs), common fermentation-producing sweeteners in the ancient Near East, not exclusively bee honey. Both leaven and honey represent fermentation / decay -- they are excluded from the altar because they symbolize corruption. The LXX renders both accurately: ζύμη (zyme, "leaven") and μέλι (meli, "honey").

13. **v.12 -- Reishit / "firstfruits":** רֵאשִׁית (reishit) means "beginning / first / firstfruits." The verse states that leavened offerings and honey may be brought near as a reishit offering -- but may not go up on the altar. This preserves a place for these items (perhaps as the firstfruits baskets of Deut 26) without burning them. The distinction between "bring near to the LORD" (general dedication) and "go up on the altar as pleasing aroma" (being burned) is important: some offerings are dedicated without being burned. The LXX renders ἀρχή (arche, "beginning / firstfruits"), a reasonable rendering.

14. **v.13 -- Melach berit Elohekha / "the salt of the covenant of your God":** This is the theological apex of the chapter. מֶלַח בְּרִית אֱלֹהֶיךָ (melach berit Elohekha) -- "the salt of the covenant of your God." Salt in the ancient Near East was a preservative, incorruptible, and a symbol of permanence and covenant fidelity. Eating salt together sealed a covenant (cf. Num 18:19, "a covenant of salt forever"; 2 Chr 13:5, "a covenant of salt"). The prohibition tashbit ("you shall not let be lacking / you shall not cause to cease") is emphatic -- salt must never be absent. The triple statement (salt it with salt / do not let the covenant salt be lacking / on all your offerings bring salt) creates an emphatic refrain. I render melach berit as "salt of the covenant" and preserve the full construction. The LXX renders ἅλα διαθήκης (hala diathekes, "salt of covenant"), a close rendering.

15. **v.14 -- Aviv qaluy ba-esh / "roasted grain crushed":** The firstfruits grain offering uses aviv (אָבִיב) -- grain in the ear, the young green grain at the stage just before full ripening, the same word used for the month of Abib (Exod 13:4). It is to be qaluy ba-esh (roasted in fire) and karmel (crushed / fresh-ground). Karmel (כַּרְמֶל) here means "fresh-roasted crushed grain" -- a word related to Carmel (vineyard / garden) but used here for a type of grain preparation. The offering of firstfruits uses the grain at its most alive and green, roasted immediately. The LXX renders ἄδραγμα νέον πεφρυγμένον (adragma neon pephrygmenon, "a new bundle, roasted"), capturing the freshness and roasting but rendering aviv as "new" (neos) rather than transliterating.

16. **v.16 -- Symmetry with v.2:** The chapter closes with a near-exact repetition of the burning formula from v.2: the priest burns the azkarah from the firstfruits offering, with oil and all its frankincense, as a fire offering to the LORD. This creates a structural envelope (inclusio) around the entire chapter: the standard grain offering (v.1-3) and the firstfruits grain offering (v.14-16) are governed by the same memorial-portion formula. The omission of re'ach nichoach ("pleasing aroma") from v.16 in the MT (the phrase ends with "a fire offering to the LORD" without the aroma qualification) is notable and may be intentional -- the Vaticanus LXX likewise lacks the full aroma formula at this verse, confirming the MT reading.

### Structural Notes

The chapter is organized in four sections:

**A. Standard grain offering of fine flour (vv.1-3):** Basic minchah -- raw fine flour with oil and frankincense; handful burned, remainder to priests.

**B. Grain offerings by cooking method (vv.4-10):** Three sub-types: oven-baked (v.4), griddle-cooked (vv.5-6), pan-cooked (v.7). All follow the same structure: make it, bring it, priest burns the memorial portion, remainder is most holy for the priests.

**C. Prohibitions and salt law (vv.11-13):** No leaven, no honey on the altar (but permitted as firstfruits dedication). Salt mandatory on all offerings -- the covenant of salt.

**D. Grain offering of firstfruits (vv.14-16):** Special category using green roasted grain (aviv), with oil and frankincense; same memorial-portion burning rite.

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary (pp. 86–87)

The Vaticanus pages (pa_0086–pa_0087) show the LXX text of Leviticus 2 in continuous Greek uncial script. The chapter is continuous with the surrounding text, without a chapter break marker visible at the opening of the chapter (the CAP marker for chapter 2 would appear at the close of chapter 1 material). Key observations from the manuscript images:

- The LXX renders nefesh as ψυχή (psuche, "soul") at v.1 -- confirming this reading is ancient and is not a later MT-only development.
- θυσία (thusia) is used throughout for minchah -- the LXX translator has generalized the term rather than creating a specific Greek equivalent.
- μνημόσυνον (mnemosynon) for azkarah is consistently rendered, preserving the "memorial/remembrance" dimension.
- The salt of the covenant formula (v.13) appears in the LXX as ἁλί ἁλισθήσεται (hali halisthesetai, "with salt it shall be salted") followed by ἅλα διαθήκης (hala diathekes, "salt of covenant") -- the figura etymologica of the Hebrew titmalach melach ("you shall salt with salt") is preserved in the Greek as a salt-cognate construction, suggesting deliberate attention by the LXX translator to the rhetorical force of the repetition.
- The LXX of v.11 includes both leaven terms (ζύμη and ζύμωσις) where the MT has se'or and chametz, distinguishing the leavening agent from the leavened result, suggesting the LXX translator understood the Hebrew distinction.
- The aviv qaluy karmel terminology in v.14 is rendered expansively in the LXX, consistent with the LXX tendency to interpret difficult technical agricultural vocabulary rather than transliterate.

— EMET
